children.name: Theraphosa blondi
children.description: World's largest spider by mass (up to 170g, 30cm leg span). Native to Venezuelan and Brazilian rainforests. Diet mainly invertebrates, frogs, and small rodents. Stridulation audible 5m away.

children.name: Theraphosa apophysis
children.description: A giant Venezuelan tarantula rivalling T. blondi in size, with distinctive pink-tipped tarsi; one of the largest spiders in the world by leg span.

children.name: Theraphosa stirmi
children.description: A massive tarantula from the Guiana Shield with a distinct burgundy carapace sheen.

children.name: Theraphosa pseudoblondi
children.description: Previously confused with T. blondi; recognised by spermathecal morphology.

children.name: Theraphosa aurantiaca
children.description: A rare orange-haired Theraphosa from Roraima, Brazil; unique colour within the genus.

children.name: Brachypelma hamorii
children.description: Iconic tarantula of western Mexico with vivid orange-red leg-joint patches. CITES II listed due to overcollection. Females may live 30 years.

children.name: Brachypelma auratum
children.description: Similar to hamorii with flame-like orange banding; restricted to Guerrero state, Mexico.

children.name: Brachypelma smithi
children.description: A docile, long-lived Mexican tarantula with orange-red leg patches, historically the classic pet-trade redknee; CITES II protected.

children.name: Brachypelma boehmei
children.description: A striking tarantula of Guerrero, Mexico, with deep red and black banded legs; popular but CITES II listed.

children.name: Brachypelma vagans
children.description: Widespread from Mexico to Colombia; common in the pet trade but CITES II listed.

children.name: Brachypelma albopilosum
children.description: A Costa Rican tarantula with a fluffy curled-hair appearance; extremely popular in the pet trade.

children.name: Brachypelma emilia
children.description: Striking orange-red carapace and dark legs; found along Mexico's Pacific coast.

children.name: Brachypelma klaasi
children.description: The rarest Brachypelma from Jalisco with a pinkish carapace; listed as Endangered.

children.name: Poecilotheria regalis
children.description: Arboreal tarantula of peninsular India. Striking geometric pattern. Fast and defensive; IUCN Endangered.

children.name: Poecilotheria metallica
children.description: Critically Endangered; known from a single forest patch near Nandyal, India. Extraordinary metallic blue colouration.

children.name: Poecilotheria ornata
children.description: A large arboreal tarantula endemic to Sri Lanka with intricate fringed patterning; potent venom and fast defensive behaviour.

children.name: Poecilotheria subfusca
children.description: A montane Sri Lankan tree spider with cream and dark banding, prized by keepers for its colouration.

children.name: Poecilotheria rufilata
children.description: A large arboreal tarantula from the Western Ghats of India, greenish with reddish leg highlights; assessed as Endangered due to habitat loss.

children.name: Poecilotheria vittata
children.description: A Sri Lankan arboreal tarantula with striped leg pattern; IUCN Vulnerable.

children.name: Poecilotheria stricta
children.description: Endemic to the Western Ghats of India with narrow leg banding; Critically Endangered.

children.name: Poecilotheria uniformis
children.description: A recently described Sri Lankan species with less contrasting leg markings.

children.name: Poecilotheria rajaei
children.description: Described from northern Sri Lanka in 2012, surviving in isolated forest fragments.

children.name: Chromatopelma cyaneopubescens
children.description: Native to arid scrubland of northern Venezuela. Adults develop metallic blue carapace, orange abdomen, green legs -- arguably the most colourful spider on Earth.

children.name: Chromatopelma eugeni
children.description: A Venezuelan species with greener carapace than C. cyaneopubescens.

children.name: Selenocosmia stirlingi
children.description: Large arid-zone tarantula documented to prey on frogs, small lizards, and juvenile snakes. Venom causes prolonged illness in dogs.

children.name: Selenocosmia crassipes
children.description: Australia's largest tarantula, named for the hissing stridulation it makes when threatened; found in tropical Queensland.

children.name: Selenocosmia fulvipes
children.description: An Australian species from Queensland with distinctive golden-coloured chelicerae.

children.name: Selenocosmia papuana
children.description: From Papua New Guinea and Torres Strait; produces stridulatory hissing when disturbed.

children.name: Grammostola rosea
children.description: A hardy, docile ground tarantula of arid Chile, one of the most common species in the pet trade.

children.name: Grammostola pulchripes
children.description: A large, calm tarantula from Paraguay and Argentina with golden leg stripes; popular beginner species.

children.name: Grammostola pulchra
children.description: A glossy jet-black, docile and long-lived tarantula from southern Brazil and Uruguay.

children.name: Grammostola actaeon
children.description: A large, hardy tarantula from southern Brazil, Uruguay, and Argentina, with a golden-brown carapace and calm temperament.

children.name: Grammostola althaepus
children.description: A large Grammostola from the Chilean Andes foothills with a coppery-rose carapace.

children.name: Grammostola chalcothrix
children.description: Named for its coppery-brown carapace sheen; females can exceed 25 years.

children.name: Grammostola quaternarius
children.description: A distinctive Grammostola from Uruguay with a four-lobed spermatheca.

children.name: Aphonopelma chalcodes
children.description: A desert tarantula of the southwestern US, with males famously wandering in late summer to find mates.

children.name: Aphonopelma hentzi
children.description: The most widespread tarantula in the United States, common across the southern Great Plains.

children.name: Aphonopelma seemanni
children.description: A Costa Rican species with black-and-white striped legs; popular for its striking pattern.

children.name: Aphonopelma anceps
children.description: A rare deep-burrowing species from the Texas panhandle.

children.name: Aphonopelma gremium
children.description: A small species from the Mojave Desert; one of the most heat-tolerant tarantulas.

children.name: Lasiodora parahybana
children.description: A very large, fast-growing Brazilian tarantula with salmon-pink hairs on the legs and abdomen; one of the biggest New World species.

children.name: Lasiodora difficilis
children.description: A very large Lasiodora from Bahia, Brazil, with reddish abdominal hairs.

children.name: Lasiodora klugei
children.description: From the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil; smaller but longer-legged than L. parahybana.

children.name: Lasiodora curvisetosa
children.description: A rarely imported Brazilian species with curved hook-like setae on its palps.

children.name: Pterinochilus murinus
children.description: A fast, defensive East African baboon spider famed among keepers for its fiery orange form and potent venom.

children.name: Pterinochilus lugardi
children.description: A striking silver-and-black baboon spider from Zimbabwe and Mozambique.

children.name: Pterinochilus cordatus
children.description: An Angolan species with a distinctive heart-shaped carapace marking.

children.name: Pterinochilus vorax
children.description: Found across East Africa from Ethiopia to Tanzania; adaptable to savanna and forest.

children.name: Cyriopagopus lividus
children.description: A burrowing Southeast Asian tarantula with iridescent cobalt-blue legs; reclusive and defensive.

children.name: Cyriopagopus albostriatus
children.description: A burrowing tarantula of Thailand with subtle pale striping, common in dry forest soils.

children.name: Cyriopagopus hartmanni
children.description: A newly described species from Myanmar with vivid metallic blue carapace.

children.name: Cyriopagopus paganus
children.description: A large semi-arboreal Cyriopagopus from the Ayeyarwady region of Myanmar.

children.name: Caribena versicolor
children.description: A spectacular arboreal tarantula from Martinique, with iridescent blue-green spiderlings maturing into pink-and-green adults; docile and popular in the hobby.

children.name: Caribena laeta
children.description: Endemic to Puerto Rico; closely related to C. versicolor but with subdued coloration.

children.name: Avicularia
children.description: Arboreal tarantulas from Central and South America with pink or reddish tarsal tips.

children.name: Avicularia avicularia
children.description: The type species, widespread across Amazonia; spiderlings have bright blue legs.

children.name: Avicularia metallica
children.description: Found in Suriname and French Guiana with a metallic blue carapace sheen.

children.name: Avicularia purpurea
children.description: An Ecuadorian species with deep violet-purple carapace; one of the most colourful Avicularia.

children.name: Avicularia juruensis
children.description: Named after the Jurua River in Brazil; golden-brown carapace with pink toes.

children.name: Avicularia velutina
children.description: A Venezuelan species with velvety black body and bright pink tarsi.

children.name: Psalmopoeus
children.description: Fast arboreal tarantulas from Central and South America lacking urticating hairs.

children.name: Psalmopoeus cambridgei
children.description: A large arboreal tarantula from Trinidad with V-shaped chevron abdomen pattern.

children.name: Psalmopoeus irminia
children.description: Stunning black and orange banding pattern; one of the most beautiful arboreal tarantulas.

children.name: Psalmopoeus pulcher
children.description: A Panamanian species with pale golden carapace hairs; the most docile Psalmopoeus.

children.name: Psalmopoeus reduncus
children.description: Endemic to Costa Rica's Pacific slope; has a distinctive orange-coloured oral region.

children.name: Heteroscodra
children.description: Old World tarantulas from West and Central Africa with potent venom.

children.name: Heteroscodra maculata
children.description: Infamous for potent neurotoxic venom causing prolonged muscle cramps; arboreal and fast.

children.name: Heteroscodra crassipes
children.description: A West African species with thickened front legs adapted for digging.

children.name: Heteroscodra sultana
children.description: The largest Heteroscodra; warm brown with subtle golden highlights.
